dominant_palette: antique gold, sterling silver, cream/ivory, deep crimson, matte black
secondary_palette: dusty lavender, powder pink, olive/army green, cobalt blue
composition_vocabulary: Almost all tiles are tight product close-ups — ring held between fingers, object centred in palm, or floating on a clean ground. No wide environmental shots; the frame is always pressed close to the object. A handful of sketch/illustration tiles break the photography with flat linework, which creates a sketchbook-meets-showroom rhythm.
lighting_quality: Predominantly polished studio or CGI-render lighting with hard specular highlights on metal; a minority of tiles use natural window light, and two use flat graphic-design light — the overall effect is controlled and high-contrast rather than soft or atmospheric.
material_textures: polished yellow gold, brushed or oxidised silver, smooth enamel, ceramic/porcelain, ink-on-paper, snakeskin/reptile texture
aesthetic_register: The sample sits squarely at the intersection of luxury-heirloom and mystic-talisman, with a deliberate occult undertow — serpents, eyes, Medusa, and the scorpion read as intentional symbolic vocabulary rather than decorative accident; the brand stationery tiles add a craft-studio identity layer.
notably_absent: Bridal or solitaire-diamond imagery, soft pastel colour palettes, lifestyle or model-on-body photography, and the aspirational-white-linen aesthetic common to most luxury-jewellery moodboards are entirely missing.
